factors.summary: Organic growth appears to be mixed, with some evidence indicating a deceleration in revenue growth, particularly in certain business segments, while other sections suggest stability or positive long-term outlook.

evidence.text: The long-term outlook for the industry continues to remain positive due to the fundamental drivers of air travel growth: economic growth and the increasing propensity to travel due to increased trade, globalization, and improved airline services driven by liberalization of air traffic rights between countries.

Revenues in 2017 decreased by $1,179 million or 1% compared with 2016. BCA revenues decreased by $1,283 million or 2% primarily due to delivery mix, with fewer twin aisle deliveries more than offsetting the impact of higher single aisle deliveries.

BGS revenues in 2017 increased by $714 million compared with 2016 primarily due to higher commercial parts revenue. BGS revenues in 2016 increased by $632 million compared with 2015 primarily due to higher commercial parts revenues and government services revenue.

factors.summary: The revenue mix is shifting, primarily due to changes in delivery mixes across different airplane segments. While there is a shift towards lower-margin segments, such as fewer twin aisle deliveries and lower milestone revenues, there are also indications of potential improvements in margins for new programs over time.

evidence.text: Commercial Airplanes revenues decreased by $1,283 million or 2% in 2017 compared with 2016 due to delivery mix, with fewer twin aisle deliveries more than offsetting the impact of higher single aisle deliveries.

BCA revenues decreased by $1,283 million or 2% primarily due to delivery mix, with fewer twin aisle deliveries more than offsetting the impact of higher single aisle deliveries. BDS revenues decreased by $1,506 million primarily due to fewer C-17 deliveries, lower milestone revenue on satellite programs, and the mix of deliveries on the Apache and F-15 programs, partially offset by higher volume on various weapons programs.

BGS earnings from operations in 2017 increased by $79 million compared with 2016 primarily due to higher revenues, partially offset by commercial services mix. Net favorable cumulative contract catch-up adjustments were $9 million lower in 2017 than in 2016.

The combined gross margin percentages for our commercial airplane programs had been estimated to be 1% higher or lower it would have a similar effect on the Commercial Airplane segment’s operating margins.

factors.summary: Overall demand for air travel and aerospace services appears to be growing, driven by economic growth and long-term trends in passenger and cargo traffic. However, there are slight decreases in certain areas, such as revenues, which may indicate some challenges or specific risks impacting demand.

evidence.text: Passenger traffic in 2017 is estimated to grow by more than 7%, exceeding the long-term trend of approximately 5%. Air cargo traffic growth, building on the recovery that started in 2016, is expected to exceed 9% in 2017.

Revenues in 2017 decreased by $1,179 million or 1% compared with 2016. BCA revenues decreased by $1,283 million or 2% primarily due to delivery mix, with fewer twin aisle deliveries more than offsetting the impact of higher single aisle deliveries.

The aerospace markets we serve include parts distribution, logistics, and other inventory services; maintenance, engineering, and upgrades; training and professional services; and information services. We expect the market to grow by around 3.5% annually.

The commercial aircraft market is predominantly driven by long-term trends in airline passenger and cargo traffic. The principal factors underlying long-term traffic growth are sustained economic growth and political stability both in developed and emerging markets. Demand for our commercial aircraft is further influenced by airline profitability, availability of aircraft financing, world trade policies, government-to-government relations, technological advances, price and other competitive factors, fuel prices, terrorism, epidemics and environmental regulations.
evidence.subsection: Our Commercial Airplanes and Global Services businesses depend heavily on commercial airlines, and are subject to unique risks.

factors.summary: Volume trends are mixed, with growth in certain regions and segments offset by declines in others. While unit volumes are growing in the Asia Pacific and Europe regions, as well as in low-cost-carrier segments, there are also reports of unit volumes declining, particularly in terms of revenues and delivery mix. Additionally, new programs like the 787-10 and 777X are contributing to growth, but production challenges and delays could impact future trends.

evidence.text: Airlines operating in the Asia Pacific regions and Europe, as well as low-cost-carriers globally, are currently leading the growth in passenger traffic.

Revenues in 2017 decreased by $1,179 million or 1% compared with 2016. BCA revenues decreased by $1,283 million or 2% primarily due to delivery mix, with fewer twin aisle deliveries more than offsetting the impact of higher single aisle deliveries.

As the size of the worldwide commercial airline fleet continues to grow, so does demand for aftermarket services designed to increase efficiency and extend the economic lives of airplanes. Airlines are using data analytics to plan flight operations and predictive maintenance to improve their productivity and efficiency.

We have plans to adjust production rates on several of our commercial aircraft programs, while at the same time engaging in significant ongoing development and production of the 787-10 and 777X aircraft.

factors.summary: The end market momentum is mixed, with growth in certain regions and markets offset by challenges in others. The commercial aviation sector faces risks related to economic conditions and geopolitical factors, while the defense markets are more stable. The Asia Pacific and Europe regions are leading passenger and cargo traffic growth, and the Middle East and Asia Pacific are driving growth in non-U.S. government services markets. However, the U.S. government services market remains the largest, with growth expected to remain flat, while non-U.S. markets, particularly Middle East and Asia Pacific, are growing fastest.

evidence.text: Airlines operating in the Asia Pacific regions and Europe, as well as low-cost-carriers globally, are currently leading the growth in passenger traffic. Air cargo traffic growth, building on the recovery that started in 2016, is expected to exceed 9% in 2017, driven by strong trade and industrial production in all regions.

While our principal operations are in the U.S., we conduct operations in an expanding number of countries and rely on an extensive network of non-U.S. partners, key suppliers and subcontractors. Our strategy is centered on successful execution in healthy core businesses – Commercial Airplanes (BCA), Defense, Space & Security (BDS), and Global Services (BGS) – supplemented and supported by Boeing Capital (BCC).

The U.S. government services market is the single largest individual market, comprising over 50 percent of the government services markets served. Over the next decade, we expect U.S. growth to remain flat and non-U.S. fleets, led by Middle East and Asia Pacific customers, to add rotorcraft and commercial derivative aircraft at the fastest rates.

factors.summary: The company is sensitive to multiple factors including fuel prices, government defense budgets, competitive pressures, aircraft fleet retirements, and various internal and external operational risks such as labor costs, production performance, and external financing challenges. While the company faces significant vulnerabilities from external market developments, it is also actively working to mitigate risks through alternative financing and customer support.

evidence.text: The industry remains vulnerable to exogenous developments including fuel price spikes, credit market shocks, acts of terrorism, natural disasters, conflicts, epidemics and increased global environmental regulations. factors.summary: The company faces several near-term risks, including potential program delays and cost overruns on key programs such as the 747 and KC-46A Tanker. Additionally, disruptions from the Export-Import Bank of the United States, particularly due to its limited approval capacity, pose risks to non-U.S. customers' financing and could lead to delayed purchases or increased funding requirements. Geopolitical factors affecting defense budgets and potential production delays further contribute to these risks.

evidence.text: Reach-forward losses of $378 million, $772 million, and $513 million were recorded related to the KC-46A Tanker in 2017, 2016, and 2015, respectively. During 2016 and 2015, we recorded reach-forward losses on the 747 program of $1,258 million and $885 million.

Many of our non-U.S. customers finance purchases through the Export-Import Bank of the United States. [...] The bank will not be able to approve any transaction totaling more than $10 million. [...] We may fund additional commitments and/or enter into new financing arrangements with customers. [...] We continue to work with our customers to mitigate risks associated with the lack of a quorum of the bank’s board of directors and assist with alternative third party financing sources.

A number of factors could cause BCC’s new business volume to increase further, including if the Export-Import Bank of the United States continues to be unable to, or does not, approve new financing transactions. Aircraft valuations could decline if significant numbers of additional aircraft, particularly types with relatively few operators, are placed out of service.

In 2016 and 2015, we recorded reach-forward losses of $1,258 million and $885 million on the 747 program. We continue to evaluate the viability of the 747 program and it is reasonably possible that we could decide to end production of the 747.

factors.summary: The company identifies new product launches, such as the 737 MAX 10 and 787-10, as key catalysts for future growth. evidence.text: We delivered the first 737 MAX 8 in May 2017 and announced the launch of the 737 MAX 10 in June 2017. First delivery of the 787-10 derivative aircraft is targeted for 2018.

factors.summary: Program execution is facing significant challenges across multiple major programs, including the 747, KC-46A Tanker, and others. These challenges include cost overruns, delays, technical issues, and potential program cancellations, which could result in financial losses and reduced profitability. The company continues to evaluate the viability of several programs, with the 747 program at risk of being discontinued due to lower demand and past financial losses. Additionally, the KC-46A Tanker program has experienced higher estimated costs and certification delays, leading to substantial reach-forward losses. These execution issues highlight the company's exposure to financial risks associated with major development programs.

evidence.text: "We continue to evaluate the viability of the 747 program and it is reasonably possible that we could decide to end production of the 747."

As with any development program, this program remains subject to additional reach-forward losses or delivery delays if we experience further production, technical or quality issues, delays in certification and/or flight testing."

factors.summary: Labor relations are mixed with some stability from defined contribution plans but risks from union representation and potential work stoppages.

evidence.text: Approximately 52,000 employees, which constitute 37% of our total workforce, were union represented as of December 31, 2017. We experienced a work stoppage in 2008 when a labor strike halted commercial aircraft and certain BDS program production. We may experience additional work stoppages in the future, which could adversely affect our business.
evidence.subsection: Some of our and our suppliers’ workforces are represented by labor unions, which may lead to work stoppages.

evidence.text: The majority of our employees have earned benefits under defined benefit pension plans. Nonunion and the majority of union employees that had participated in defined benefit pension plans transitioned to a company-funded defined contribution retirement savings plan in 2016.

factors.summary: International sales are significant and growing, particularly in the Middle East and Asia Pacific regions. However, there are risks associated with financing challenges and geopolitical factors, which could impact the stability and future growth of these sales. The company is exposed to economic and political risks in non-U.S. markets, and international defense sales are stable but face potential delays due to financing constraints. Additionally, the reliance on export financing through the Export-Import Bank introduces vulnerabilities, as its operational limitations could affect the ability to secure financing for non-U.S. customers, potentially delaying purchases and impacting future sales campaigns. Despite these risks, the strong backlog and expected expansion in the international sales pipeline suggest a positive outlook for international sales in the near term, though caution is advised due to the mentioned challenges.

evidence.text: Approximately 91% of Commercial Airplanes’ total backlog, in dollar terms, is with non-U.S. airlines.

In 2017, non-U.S. customers, which includes FMS, accounted for approximately 55% of our revenues. We expect that non-U.S. sales will continue to account for a significant portion of our revenues for the foreseeable future.
Over the next decade, we expect non-U.S. fleets, led by Middle East and Asia Pacific customers, to add rotorcraft and commercial derivative aircraft at the fastest rates.
evidence.subsection: We derive a significant portion of our revenues from non-U.S. sales and are subject to the risks of doing business in other countries.

evidence.text: Many of our non-U.S. customers finance purchases through the Export-Import Bank of the United States. Following the expiration of the bank’s charter on June 30, 2015, the bank’s charter was reauthorized in December 2015. The bank is now authorized through September 30, 2019. However, until the U.S. Senate confirms members sufficient to reconstitute a quorum of the bank’s board of directors, the bank will not be able to approve any transaction totaling more than $10 million. As a result, we may fund additional commitments and/or enter into new financing arrangements with customers. Certain of our non-U.S. customers also may seek to delay purchases if they cannot obtain financing at reasonable costs, and there may be further impacts with respect to future sales campaigns involving non-U.S. customers. We continue to work with our customers to mitigate risks associated with the lack of a quorum of the bank’s board of directors and assist with alternative third party financing sources.

factors.summary: The company faces potential material impacts from litigation and government inquiries, which could affect its financial position and results. Legal contingencies are discussed in Note 20 to the Consolidated Financial Statements.

evidence.text: The outcome of litigation and of government inquiries and investigations involving our business is unpredictable and an adverse decision in any such matter could have a material effect on our financial position and results of operations.
evidence.subsection: Unauthorized access to our or our customers’ information and systems could negatively impact our business.

evidence.text: Legal contingencies are discussed in Note 20 to our Consolidated Financial Statements.

evidence.text: Following the expiration of the bank’s charter on June 30, 2015, the bank’s charter was reauthorized in December 2015. The bank is now authorized through September 30, 2019.
